title: CLAUDE.md
text: file provides guidance Claude Code (claude.ai/code) working code repository.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: skmle R package implementing Sieve Maximum Kernel-weighted Log-likelihood Estimator (SMKLE) Sun et al. 2025 (JASA) transformed hazards models sparse, intermittently observed longitudinal covariates. heavy lifting C++ backend linked via Rcpp + RcppArmadillo, nloptr’s C API used optimizer.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Use devtools package root: devtools::load_all() — compile C++ load package interactive work. devtools::document() — regenerate man/*.Rd NAMESPACE roxygen comments. Always run editing roxygen blocks. Rcpp::compileAttributes() — regenerate src/RcppExports.cpp R/RcppExports.R adding/changing // [[Rcpp::export]] signatures. Must run document() exported C++ prototypes change. devtools::install() / R CMD INSTALL . — full install. R CMD build . && R CMD check skmle_*.tar.gz — full CRAN-style check.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: src/Makevars resolves nloptr include dir build time via Rscript -e "cat(system.file('include', package='nloptr'))". src/Makevars.win hardcodes Windows path — regenerate Windows machine source("dev/gen_makevars.R") local nloptr install path changes. Never edit src/Makevars.win hand changing versions.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: testthat suite yet. Correctness verified hand via scripts tests/ compare packaged implementation pure-R prototype. script expects run project root (paths like dev/Rprototype_funcs.R relative). Scripts source dev/Rprototype_funcs.R (ground-truth R prototypes) tests/multiple_Cao_*_funcs.R (per-model references), run side--side print .equal comparisons. Typical flow, package root R: source("tests/verify_skmle.R"), source("tests/verify_kee_cox.R"), source("tests/verify_kee_additive.R"), source("tests/verify_skmle_cv.R"), source("tests/test_plot.R"). tests/verify_0.R re-uses fitted object verify_skmle.R sanity-check NLL maxeval = 1. dev/ .Rbuildignore .gitignore — prototype files live outside built package required verify scripts run.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: three estimator wrappers (skmle(), kee_cox(), kee_additive() R/skmle.R, R/kee.R) share front-end pattern: Parse formula via stats::model.frame using manual match.call() + mf_call trick id obs_times carried alongside Surv response NA-synchronised . Drop (Intercept) column model.matrix. Intercept-models explicitly rejected. Coerce id integer codes via .integer(factor(id_vec)) — non-numeric identifiers supported . Compute Epanechnikov kernel kerval = (1 - u^2) * 0.75 / h u = (X - obs_times)/h, zeroed X <= obs_times (past observations inform subject’s likelihood contribution). Delegate // [[Rcpp::export]] function src/ via thin R/RcppExports.R bridge. Never call .Call directly. Post-process: assemble sandwich variance /B (W/Sigma) matrices returned C++, wrap S3 class (skmle, kee, cv.skmle).

title: CLAUDE.md
text: skmle() precomputes B-spline sieve (splines::ns equally spaced interior knots (1:nknots)/(nknots+1) [0,1]) baseline hazard, plus Legendre-Gauss quadrature nodes gaussquad cumulative-hazard integral. Two basis matrices built: bsmat event/censoring times X, bsmat_tt_all mapped quadrature nodes 0.5*lq_x + 0.5, along kerval_tt_all = kernel values quadrature node every observation time. s != 0 (non-Cox transformations), ineqmat nloptr inequality constraints built rows 0 < X - obs_times <= h; enforces s*(Z'β + B'γ) + 1 > 0 inside Box-Cox transform. src/skmle_cpp.cpp defines trans_fun, trans_fun_d, etc. Box-Cox family s = 0 special-cased exp. calc_A / calc_B (called back C++) form Godambe sandwich ^{-1} B ^{-1} / n.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: kee_cox() solves partial-likelihood-style kernel estimating equation via nleqslv::nleqslv; variance comes kee_cox_var returning W, Sigma assembled W^{-1} Σ W^{-1}. kee_additive() closed-form — kee_additive_est returns estimate plus A_est, B_est, Sigma_est one C++ call.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: skmle_cv() (R/skmle_cv.R, exported) generates automatic log-spaced h_grid [max(min(pos_diffs), n^-0.6), min(max(max_diffs), n^-0.3)], assigns folds subject id (row), delegates whole K×|h_grid| loop skmle_cv_cpp one call — C++ side refits train split evaluates skmle_eval_nll_cpp held-subjects. selecting h_cv, refits full data rewriting call[[1]] skmle::skmle stripping CV-arguments.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: sim_skmle_data() (R/simulate.R) draws bivariate covariate (step-function AR-like process + binary indicator), inverts transformed cumulative hazard via Legendre-Gauss quadrature + nleqslv, draws observation times thinned NHPP (NonHPP_gen). two covariates supported — length(beta) must 2.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Outer loops src/skmle_cpp.cpp src/kee.cpp call Rcpp::checkUserInterrupt() every ~100 iterations. Preserve adding new loops Ctrl-C still works.

title: Benchmarking skmle Against SurvSparse
text: vignette benchmarks skmle SurvSparse package sparse longitudinal survival-data setting. compare: SurvSparse::add.haz() skmle::kee_additive() skmle::skmle(s = 1). SurvSparse::trans.haz() skmle::skmle(s = 0). goal claim universal speed ratio every problem size, show package behaves across small grid representative settings.
code: library(nloptr) library(skmle) library(SurvSparse) library(survival) library(dplyr) library(bench) library(ggplot2)

title: Benchmarking skmle Against SurvSparse
text: simulate sparse longitudinal survival data using sim_skmle_data(). fair comparison SurvSparse, expects single longitudinal covariate benchmark calls, use first simulated covariate.
code: make_benchmark_data <- function(n, s_val) {   dat <- sim_skmle_data(     n = n,     mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),     mu_bar = 8,     alpha = function(tt) 0.5 * 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),     beta = c(1, -0.5),     s = s_val,     cen = 1.0   )    dat %>%     dplyr::rename(covariates_old = covariates) %>%     dplyr::mutate(       covariates = covariates_old[, 1],       X = X,       delta = delta,       obs_times = obs_times,       id = as.integer(factor(id))     ) %>%     dplyr::select(id, X, covariates, obs_times, delta) %>%     as.data.frame() }

title: Benchmarking skmle Against SurvSparse
code: run_additive_benchmark <- function(n, iterations = 5) {   dat <- make_benchmark_data(n, s_val = 1)   h_val <- n^(-0.5)    res <- bench::mark(     SurvSparse_add_haz = add.haz(       data = dat, n = n, tau = 1, h = h_val, method = 3     ),     skmle_kee_additive = kee_additive(       Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,       data = dat, id = id, obs_times = obs_times, h = h_val     ),     skmle_spline = skmle(       Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,       data = dat, id = id, obs_times = obs_times,       s = 1, h = h_val, nknots = 3, norder = 3     ),     iterations = iterations,     check = FALSE   )    as.data.frame(res[, c("expression", "median", "itr/sec")]) %>%     dplyr::mutate(expression = as.character(expression)) %>%     dplyr::mutate(       scenario = sprintf("Additive (n = %d)", n),       median_ms = as.numeric(median) / 1e6     ) %>%     dplyr::select(scenario, expression, median_ms, `itr/sec`) }  run_transformed_benchmark <- function(n, iterations = 5) {   dat <- make_benchmark_data(n, s_val = 0)   h_val <- n^(-0.5)    res <- bench::mark(     SurvSparse_trans_haz = trans.haz(       data = dat, n = n, nknots = 3, norder = 3, tau = 1, s = 0, h = h_val     ),     skmle_spline = skmle(       Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,       data = dat, id = id, obs_times = obs_times,       s = 0, h = h_val, nknots = 3, norder = 3     ),     iterations = iterations,     check = FALSE   )    as.data.frame(res[, c("expression", "median", "itr/sec")]) %>%     dplyr::mutate(expression = as.character(expression)) %>%     dplyr::mutate(       scenario = sprintf("Transformed (n = %d)", n),       median_ms = as.numeric(median) / 1e6     ) %>%     dplyr::select(scenario, expression, median_ms, `itr/sec`) }

title: Benchmarking skmle Against SurvSparse
text: make speed comparison easier read, next table reports ratio relative skmle method interest scenario.
code: set.seed(20260325)  benchmark_results <- dplyr::bind_rows(   run_additive_benchmark(200, iterations = 5),   run_additive_benchmark(500, iterations = 5),   run_transformed_benchmark(100, iterations = 5),   run_transformed_benchmark(200, iterations = 5) )  benchmark_results <- benchmark_results %>%   dplyr::mutate(     method = dplyr::recode(       expression,       SurvSparse_add_haz = "SurvSparse add.haz",       SurvSparse_trans_haz = "SurvSparse trans.haz",       skmle_kee_additive = "skmle kee_additive",       skmle_spline = "skmle spline"     )   )  benchmark_results #>                 scenario           expression    median_ms    itr/sec #> 1     Additive (n = 200)   SurvSparse_add_haz 3.274693e-07  3.6229746 #> 2     Additive (n = 200)   skmle_kee_additive 8.979038e-08 10.9835866 #> 3     Additive (n = 200)         skmle_spline 1.151824e-07  8.5043567 #> 4     Additive (n = 500)   SurvSparse_add_haz 6.339809e-07  1.3839274 #> 5     Additive (n = 500)   skmle_kee_additive 9.490045e-08 10.5569238 #> 6     Additive (n = 500)         skmle_spline 1.431386e-07  6.8995719 #> 7  Transformed (n = 100) SurvSparse_trans_haz 5.170792e-07  1.9505868 #> 8  Transformed (n = 100)         skmle_spline 9.701162e-08 10.0422958 #> 9  Transformed (n = 200) SurvSparse_trans_haz 1.016178e-06  0.9673586 #> 10 Transformed (n = 200)         skmle_spline 1.075795e-07  9.1597049 #>                  method #> 1    SurvSparse add.haz #> 2    skmle kee_additive #> 3          skmle spline #> 4    SurvSparse add.haz #> 5    skmle kee_additive #> 6          skmle spline #> 7  SurvSparse trans.haz #> 8          skmle spline #> 9  SurvSparse trans.haz #> 10         skmle spline baseline_rows <- benchmark_results %>%   dplyr::filter(     (grepl("^Additive", scenario) & expression == "skmle_kee_additive") |       (grepl("^Transformed", scenario) & expression == "skmle_spline")   ) %>%   dplyr::transmute(scenario, baseline_ms = median_ms)  speed_summary <- benchmark_results %>%   dplyr::left_join(baseline_rows, by = "scenario") %>%   dplyr::mutate(speedup_vs_baseline = median_ms / baseline_ms)  speed_summary #>                 scenario           expression    median_ms    itr/sec #> 1     Additive (n = 200)   SurvSparse_add_haz 3.274693e-07  3.6229746 #> 2     Additive (n = 200)   skmle_kee_additive 8.979038e-08 10.9835866 #> 3     Additive (n = 200)         skmle_spline 1.151824e-07  8.5043567 #> 4     Additive (n = 500)   SurvSparse_add_haz 6.339809e-07  1.3839274 #> 5     Additive (n = 500)   skmle_kee_additive 9.490045e-08 10.5569238 #> 6     Additive (n = 500)         skmle_spline 1.431386e-07  6.8995719 #> 7  Transformed (n = 100) SurvSparse_trans_haz 5.170792e-07  1.9505868 #> 8  Transformed (n = 100)         skmle_spline 9.701162e-08 10.0422958 #> 9  Transformed (n = 200) SurvSparse_trans_haz 1.016178e-06  0.9673586 #> 10 Transformed (n = 200)         skmle_spline 1.075795e-07  9.1597049 #>                  method  baseline_ms speedup_vs_baseline #> 1    SurvSparse add.haz 8.979038e-08            3.647042 #> 2    skmle kee_additive 8.979038e-08            1.000000 #> 3          skmle spline 8.979038e-08            1.282792 #> 4    SurvSparse add.haz 9.490045e-08            6.680484 #> 5    skmle kee_additive 9.490045e-08            1.000000 #> 6          skmle spline 9.490045e-08            1.508303 #> 7  SurvSparse trans.haz 9.701162e-08            5.330075 #> 8          skmle spline 9.701162e-08            1.000000 #> 9  SurvSparse trans.haz 1.075795e-07            9.445826 #> 10         skmle spline 1.075795e-07            1.000000

title: Benchmarking skmle Against SurvSparse
text: first plot shows median runtime milliseconds method scenario.  second plot normalizes scenario skmle method interest: additive scenarios use kee_additive() baseline transformed scenarios use skmle(s = 0) baseline Values greater 1 indicate slower methods skmle baseline scenario.
code: ggplot(   benchmark_results,   aes(x = scenario, y = median_ms, fill = method) ) +   geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +   labs(     x = NULL,     y = "Median runtime (ms)",     fill = NULL,     title = "Benchmark runtime across representative scenarios"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(     legend.position = "top",     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)   ) ggplot(   speed_summary,   aes(x = scenario, y = speedup_vs_baseline, color = method, group = method) ) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +   geom_point(size = 3) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(     x = NULL,     y = "Relative runtime vs. skmle baseline",     color = NULL,     title = "Relative speed comparison by scenario"   ) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(     legend.position = "top",     axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)   )

title: Benchmarking skmle Against SurvSparse
text: benchmark shows stable pattern: additive comparison, kee_additive() substantially faster SurvSparse::add.haz(). transformed hazards comparison, skmle(s = 0) much faster SurvSparse::trans.haz() runs. general spline-based skmle(s = 1) fit slower kee_additive(), expected solves broader joint optimization problem rather specialized estimating equation. short, package-level Rcpp implementation preserves prototype method moving computational bottlenecks pure R.

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: skmle fits transformed hazards survival models longitudinal covariates observed sparsely intermittently time. package currently provides three main user-facing workflows: skmle() general transformed hazards model. kee_cox() proportional hazards estimating-equation approach. kee_additive() additive hazards estimating-equation approach. vignette shows usual modeling workflow: simulate data, fit model, inspect summary output, plot estimated baseline component, select bandwidth cross-validation.
code: library(nloptr) library(skmle) library(survival)

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: simulated data stored long format. row corresponds one observed longitudinal measurement time one subject. key columns : id: subject identifier X: observed event censoring time delta: event indicator covariates: observed covariate values visit time obs_times: longitudinal observation time

what: Fit the General Transformed Hazards Model
title: Getting Started with skmle
text: covariates column returned sim_skmle_data() matrix. can either use directly formula split separate columns. Using matrix directly convenient routine work. printed object gives fitted coefficients. many R model objects, formatted inferential output produced summary(). summary table reports: coefficient estimates standard errors z statistics p-values
code: fit_skmle <- skmle(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   s = 0,   h = 0.5,   nknots = 3,   norder = 3 )  fit_skmle #> Call: #> skmle(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat, id = id,  #>     obs_times = obs_times, s = 0, h = 0.5, nknots = 3, norder = 3) #>  #> Coefficients: #> covariates1 covariates2  #>   0.9215573  -0.5463503 summary(fit_skmle) #> Call: #> skmle(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat, id = id,  #>     obs_times = obs_times, s = 0, h = 0.5, nknots = 3, norder = 3) #>  #>   n= 80 #>  #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    #> covariates1  0.92156    0.31342  2.9403 0.003279 ** #> covariates2 -0.54635    0.34045 -1.6048 0.108543    #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Log-likelihood: -0.0753

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: plot visualizes estimated nonparametric baseline component sieve fit.
code: plot(fit_skmle)

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: model interest matches one specialized settings, package also provides dedicated estimating-equation estimators.

title: Getting Started with skmle
code: fit_kee_cox <- kee_cox(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   h = 0.5 )  summary(fit_kee_cox) #> Call: #> kee_cox(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat, id = id,  #>     obs_times = obs_times, h = 0.5) #>  #>   n= 80 #>  #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    #> covariates1  0.85832    0.29253  2.9341 0.003345 ** #> covariates2 -0.49833    0.34661 -1.4377 0.150516    #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: additive hazards estimator, simulate data s = 1.
code: set.seed(456)  dat_add <- sim_skmle_data(   n = 80,   mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),   mu_bar = 8,   alpha = function(tt) 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),   beta = c(1, -0.5),   s = 1,   cen = 0.7 )  fit_kee_add <- kee_additive(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat_add,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   h = 0.5 )  summary(fit_kee_add) #> Call: #> kee_additive(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat_add,  #>     id = id, obs_times = obs_times, h = 0.5) #>  #>   n= 80 #>  #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   #> covariates1  1.19769    0.52669  2.2740  0.02297 * #> covariates2  0.26643    0.68259  0.3903  0.69630   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: Bandwidth selection can handled skmle_cv(). returned object contains: selected bandwidth refitted skmle model bandwidth cross-validation loss table can inspect final refit usual way.

title: Getting Started with skmle
text: routine use, usual sequence : Prepare data long format one row per observation time. Fit skmle() want general transformed hazards model. Use summary() plot() inspect fitted model. Use skmle_cv() want data-driven bandwidth selection. Use kee_cox() kee_additive() scientific model matches specialized estimators. gives standard R model-fitting interface keeping core numerical work Rcpp backend.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Dayu Sun. Author, maintainer, copyright holder.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Sun D (2026). skmle: Sieve Kernel Maximum Likelihood Estimation Survival Models. R package version 0.1.0, https://www.sundayu./skmle/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {skmle: Sieve Kernel Maximum Likelihood Estimation for Survival Models},   author = {Dayu Sun},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://www.sundayu.me/skmle/}, }

title: skmle
text: skmle fits transformed hazards survival models longitudinal covariates observed sparsely intermittently time. package combines kernel weighting sieve maximum likelihood estimation make semiparametric regression feasible settings full covariate trajectory observed. core numerical work implemented C++ Rcpp RcppArmadillo, user-facing interface follows ordinary R modeling conventions expensive likelihood estimating-equation calculations run compiled code. model class indexed Box-Cox transformation parameter s. practice: s = 0 corresponds proportional hazards model. s = 1 corresponds additive hazards model. values s interpolate within transformed hazards family. one main motivations skmle: provides single interface broader transformed hazards family rather treating proportional additive hazards completely separate modeling frameworks.

title: skmle
text: skmle(): fit general transformed hazards model sieve maximum kernel-weighted likelihood. skmle_cv(): choose kernel bandwidth subject-level cross-validation refit final model. kee_cox(): fit proportional hazards estimating-equation model. kee_additive(): fit additive hazards estimating-equation model. sim_skmle_data(): simulate sparse longitudinal survival data long format.

title: skmle
text: Install development version GitHub: package compiles C++ code, need working toolchain Rtools Windows Xcode command line tools macOS.
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("dayusun/skmle")

title: skmle
text: returned objects use standard R model style: print() shows call fitted coefficients. summary() reports estimates, standard errors, z statistics, p-values. plot() available skmle fits visualize estimated baseline component. want specialized estimator instead full SMKLE fit: Bandwidth selection available skmle_cv():
code: library(skmle) library(survival)  set.seed(123)  dat <- sim_skmle_data(   n = 200,   mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),   mu_bar = 8,   alpha = function(tt) 0.5 * 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),   beta = c(1, -0.5),   s = 0,   # Box-Cox transformation parameter; s = 0 is proportional hazards   cen = 0.7 )  fit <- skmle(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   s = 0,   # proportional hazards model   h = 0.5,   nknots = 3 )  fit summary(fit) plot(fit) fit_cox <- kee_cox(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   h = 0.5 )  summary(fit_cox) cv_fit <- skmle_cv(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   s = 0,   # proportional hazards model   K = 3,   h_grid = c(0.3, 0.4, 0.5),   quiet = TRUE )  cv_fit$h_cv cv_fit$cv_results summary(cv_fit$fit)

title: skmle
text: package includes benchmark vignette comparing skmle SurvSparse matched sparse longitudinal survival-data settings. current benchmark sweep: additive comparison also includes general spline-based skmle(s = 1) fit, remains competitive slower kee_additive() solves broader joint optimization problem rather specialized estimating equation. full code benchmark setup, see vignette: package tutorial, see:
code: vignette("benchmark_survsparse", package = "skmle") vignette("tutorial", package = "skmle")

title: skmle
text: transformed hazards model used skmle indexed Box-Cox parameter s. parameter determines hazard-scale model linked linear predictor nonparametric baseline component. user perspective, key interpretation : choose s = 0 want proportional hazards model; choose s = 1 want additive hazards model; choose another fixed value s application calls intermediate transformed hazards specification. gives coherent way compare fit related hazard models within one estimation framework, using long-format sparse longitudinal data structure.

title: skmle
text: Sun, D., Sun, Z., Zhao, X., & Cao, H. (2025). Kernel Meets Sieve: Transformed Hazards Models Sparse Longitudinal Covariates. Journal American Statistical Association, 120(552), 2580-2591. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2025.2476781

what: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
text: Fit additive hazards model sparse longitudinal covariate data using kernel estimating-equation approach.

title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
code: kee_additive(formula, data, id, obs_times, h, lq_nodes = 64)

title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
text: formula model formula survival::Surv() response. data Data frame containing variables used fit. id Subject identifier aligned row-wise data. obs_times Longitudinal observation times aligned row-wise data. h Positive kernel bandwidth. lq_nodes Number quadrature nodes used numerical integration step.

title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
text: object class kee containing coefficient estimates, estimated variance-covariance matrix, intermediate matrices used sandwich variance estimation, model metadata.

title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
text: kee_additive() specialized additive-hazards counterpart kee_cox(). uses kernel-smoothed martingale estimating equation typically runs faster general skmle(s = 1) fit solves specialized problem.

title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
text: Sun, Dayu, Zhuowei Sun, Xingqiu Zhao, Hongyuan Cao. "Kernel Meets Sieve: Transformed Hazards Models Sparse Longitudinal Covariates." Journal American Statistical Association (2025): 1-12. Sun, Dayu, Hongyuan Cao, Yining Chen. "Additive hazards models sparse longitudinal covariates." Lifetime Data Analysis (2022).

title: Fit an Additive Hazards KEE Model — kee_additive
code: # \donttest{ library(survival)  set.seed(123) dat <- sim_skmle_data(   n = 80,   mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),   mu_bar = 8,   alpha = function(tt) 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),   beta = c(1, -0.5),   s = 1,   cen = 0.7 )  fit_add <- kee_additive(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   h = 0.5 )  summary(fit_add) #> Call: #> kee_additive(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat,  #>     id = id, obs_times = obs_times, h = 0.5) #>  #>   n= 80 #>  #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   #> covariates1  1.26747    0.66610  1.9028  0.05706 . #> covariates2 -0.54733    0.75574 -0.7242  0.46892   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 # }

what: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
text: Fit proportional hazards model sparse longitudinal covariate data using kernel estimating-equation approach.

title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
code: kee_cox(formula, data, id, obs_times, h)

title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
text: formula model formula survival::Surv() response. data Data frame containing variables used fit. id Subject identifier aligned row-wise data. obs_times Longitudinal observation times aligned row-wise data. h Positive kernel bandwidth.

title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
text: object class kee containing coefficient estimates, estimated variance-covariance matrix, estimating-equation matrices, convergence status, original function call.

title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
text: kee_cox() targets proportional hazards case without estimating nonparametric baseline component. therefore useful specialized alternative skmle() scientific model Cox-type main interest regression coefficients.

title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
text: Sun, Dayu, Zhuowei Sun, Xingqiu Zhao, Hongyuan Cao. "Kernel Meets Sieve: Transformed Hazards Models Sparse Longitudinal Covariates." Journal American Statistical Association (2025): 1-12. Cao, Hongyuan, et al. "Inference Cox models sparse longitudinal covariates." Biometrika (2015).

title: Fit a Cox-Type KEE Model — kee_cox
code: # \donttest{ library(survival)  set.seed(123) dat <- sim_skmle_data(   n = 80,   mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),   mu_bar = 8,   alpha = function(tt) 0.5 * 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),   beta = c(1, -0.5),   s = 0,   cen = 0.7 )  fit_cox <- kee_cox(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   h = 0.5 )  summary(fit_cox) #> Call: #> kee_cox(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat, id = id,  #>     obs_times = obs_times, h = 0.5) #>  #>   n= 80 #>  #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    #> covariates1  0.85832    0.29253  2.9341 0.003345 ** #> covariates2 -0.49833    0.34661 -1.4377 0.150516    #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 # }

what: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
title: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
text: Simulate right-censored survival data sparsely intermittently observed longitudinal covariates transformed hazards model.

title: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
code: sim_skmle_data(n, mu, mu_bar, alpha, beta, s, cen, nstep = 20)

title: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
text: n Number subjects simulate. mu Function giving intensity observation-time process longitudinal covariates. mu_bar Numeric upper bound mu follow-interval. alpha Function giving baseline component transformed hazards model. beta Numeric vector true regression coefficients. current simulator supports exactly two covariates, length(beta) must equal 2. s Box-Cox transformation parameter. Use s = 0 proportional hazards model s = 1 additive hazards model. cen Positive censoring parameter controlling follow-window. nstep Number grid intervals used generate latent piecewise-constant time-varying covariate process.

title: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
text: tibble long format columns: id: subject identifier. X: observed event censoring time. delta: event indicator. covariates: two-column matrix observed covariates obs_times. obs_times: longitudinal observation time. censoring: subject-specific censoring time.

title: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
text: simulated data returned long format, one row per subject-visit pair. row contains observed event censoring time, event indicator, observation time longitudinal covariate, covariate values observed visit. first covariate generated latent step function time. second covariate binary subject-level indicator. Event times generated numerically inverting cumulative transformed hazard, observation times generated non-homogeneous Poisson process.

title: Simulate Sparse Longitudinal Survival Data — sim_skmle_data
code: set.seed(123) sim_data <- sim_skmle_data(   n = 50,   mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),   mu_bar = 8,   alpha = function(tt) 0.5 * 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),   beta = c(1, -0.5),   s = 0,   cen = 0.7 )  head(sim_data) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   id        X delta covariates[,1]  [,2] obs_times censoring #>   <chr> <dbl> <lgl>          <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1     0.805 TRUE         -0.714      0     0.129     0.930 #> 2 1     0.805 TRUE         -0.916      0     0.217     0.930 #> 3 1     0.805 TRUE         -0.916      0     0.247     0.930 #> 4 1     0.805 TRUE         -0.387      0     0.433     0.930 #> 5 1     0.805 TRUE         -0.0782     0     0.798     0.930 #> 6 2     0.504 TRUE          0.402      1     0.288     0.865 table(sim_data$delta) #>  #> FALSE  TRUE  #>    49   268   # The `covariates` column is a matrix; use it directly in model formulas # or split it into explicit columns if preferred. sim_data$Z1 <- sim_data$covariates[, 1] sim_data$Z2 <- sim_data$covariates[, 2] head(sim_data[, c("id", "X", "delta", "obs_times", "Z1", "Z2")]) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   id        X delta obs_times      Z1    Z2 #>   <chr> <dbl> <lgl>     <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 1     0.805 TRUE      0.129 -0.714      0 #> 2 1     0.805 TRUE      0.217 -0.916      0 #> 3 1     0.805 TRUE      0.247 -0.916      0 #> 4 1     0.805 TRUE      0.433 -0.387      0 #> 5 1     0.805 TRUE      0.798 -0.0782     0 #> 6 2     0.504 TRUE      0.288  0.402      1

what: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
text: Fit transformed hazards model survival data sparsely intermittently observed longitudinal covariates using sieve maximum kernel-weighted log-likelihood estimator (SMKLE).

title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
code: skmle(   formula,   data,   id,   obs_times,   s,   h,   nknots = 3,   norder = 3,   lq_nodes = 64,   maxeval = 10000,   xtol_rel = 1e-06 )

title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
text: formula model formula. left-hand side must survival::Surv() response right-hand side must contain least one covariate. data Data frame containing variables used formula, id, obs_times. id Subject identifier. Non-numeric identifiers allowed internally converted integer subject codes. obs_times Longitudinal observation times aligned row-wise data. s Box-Cox transformation parameter. s = 0 gives proportional hazards model s = 1 gives additive hazards model. h Positive kernel bandwidth. nknots Number interior knots used sieve approximation baseline component. norder Order parameter supplied high-level interface spline approximation. lq_nodes Number Legendre-Gauss quadrature nodes used numerical integration. maxeval Maximum number optimizer evaluations. xtol_rel Relative convergence tolerance passed optimizer.

title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
text: object class skmle containing: coefficients: regression coefficient estimates, var: estimated variance-covariance matrix regression coefficients, gamma: estimated spline coefficients baseline component, loglik: maximized log-likelihood, convergence: optimizer status code, model metadata n, s, h, call.

title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
text: skmle() main model-fitting function package. combines: kernel weighting handle intermittently observed longitudinal covariates, sieve approximation unknown baseline component, C++-backed numerical optimizer joint estimation problem. returned object follows usual R model pattern: print fitted coefficients print(), obtain inferential output summary(), visualize estimated baseline component plot().

title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
text: Sun, Dayu, Zhuowei Sun, Xingqiu Zhao, Hongyuan Cao. "Kernel Meets Sieve: Transformed Hazards Models Sparse Longitudinal Covariates." Journal American Statistical Association (2025): 1-12.

title: Fit a Transformed Hazards Model by SMKLE — skmle
code: # \donttest{ library(survival)  set.seed(123) dat <- sim_skmle_data(   n = 80,   mu = function(tt) 8 * (0.75 + (0.5 - tt)^2),   mu_bar = 8,   alpha = function(tt) 0.5 * 0.75 + 0.75 * (tt * (1 - sin(2 * pi * (tt - 0.25)))),   beta = c(1, -0.5),   s = 0,   cen = 0.7 )  fit <- skmle(   Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates,   data = dat,   id = id,   obs_times = obs_times,   s = 0,   h = 0.5,   nknots = 3 )  summary(fit) #> Call: #> skmle(formula = Surv(X, delta) ~ covariates, data = dat, id = id,  #>     obs_times = obs_times, s = 0, h = 0.5, nknots = 3) #>  #>   n= 80 #>  #>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    #> covariates1  0.92156    0.31342  2.9403 0.003279 ** #> covariates2 -0.54635    0.34045 -1.6048 0.108543    #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Log-likelihood: -0.0753  # }

what: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv
title: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv
text: Perform K-fold cross-validation select kernel bandwidth skmle().

title: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv
code: skmle_cv(   formula,   data,   id,   obs_times,   s,   K = 5,   h_grid = NULL,   n_h = 10,   nknots = 3,   norder = 3,   lq_nodes = 64,   maxeval = 10000,   xtol_rel = 1e-06,   seed = NULL,   quiet = FALSE )

title: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv
text: formula model formula. left-hand side must survival::Surv() response right-hand side must contain least one covariate. data Data frame containing variables used formula, id, obs_times. id Subject identifier. Non-numeric identifiers allowed internally converted integer subject codes. obs_times Longitudinal observation times aligned row-wise data. s Box-Cox transformation parameter. s = 0 gives proportional hazards model s = 1 gives additive hazards model. K Number folds. h_grid Optional numeric vector candidate bandwidth values. NULL, grid generated automatically observed time gaps. n_h Number candidate bandwidths generate h_grid NULL. nknots Number interior knots used sieve approximation baseline component. norder Order parameter supplied high-level interface spline approximation. lq_nodes Number Legendre-Gauss quadrature nodes used numerical integration. maxeval Maximum number optimizer evaluations. xtol_rel Relative convergence tolerance passed optimizer. seed Optional integer seed random subject--fold assignment. NULL, current RNG state used explicit seed set. quiet Logical; TRUE, suppress progress output.

title: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv
text: object class cv.skmle components: h_cv: selected bandwidth, fit: skmle fit refit full data, cv_results: data frame candidate bandwidths CV losses, h_grid: bandwidth grid used search, fold_id: subject--fold assignment vector (length n), seed: value seed supplied user, NULL.

title: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv
text: skmle_cv() splits subjects, rows, across folds. appropriate unit cross-validation multiple rows belong subject long-format data structure. choosing bandwidth smallest average validation loss, function refits skmle() full data set using selected value. fold assignment random, pass seed (set.seed() calling) make grid selection reproducible.

title: Select the Bandwidth by Cross-Validation — skmle_cv

title: skmle 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN release. skmle() fits transformed hazards models sieve maximum kernel-weighted log-likelihood estimation (SMKLE). kee_cox() kee_additive() fit Cox additive hazards models via kernel-weighted estimating equations. skmle_cv() selects kernel bandwidth K-fold cross-validation. sim_skmle_data() simulates survival data sparse, intermittently observed longitudinal covariates. plot(), print() summary() methods fitted objects.
